text: The laparoscopic camera provides a view into the right lower quadrant, revealing free fluid that is suctioned out. Dense adhesions are visible between the appendix, large bowel, small bowel, and reproductive organs, including the ovary and fallopian tubes.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to gently break up dense adhesions surrounding the appendix, carefully avoiding injury to the inflamed bowel.
The surgeon enters an abscess, with visible pus being suctioned to minimize contamination within the patient's abdomen during the laparoscopic appendectomy.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a blunt dissecting tool to separate tissues, carefully avoiding grasping inflamed tissue to prevent injury, although it may be necessary in challenging cases.
The surgeon follows the appendix down to the cecum, deviating from the usual method of tracing the tinea. During the dissection, the area of perforation is identified and highlighted on freeze frame.
The surgeon mobilizes the right colon by taking down the white line of Toldt, medializing the colon to improve access to the appendiceal base and prevent ureteral injuries.

The surgeon uses an energy device as a dissecting instrument to enhance operational efficiency. Once mobilization is complete, the appendix is lifted to tent up the cecum, allowing evaluation of the distance between the appendiceal base and the terminal ileum, creating the critical view of safety.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the appendiceal base, ensuring the adequacy of the appendectomy to prevent stump appendicitis and avoiding occlusion at the terminal ileum. Small bites are taken to meticulously separate the appendix from surrounding bowel tissue without causing injury.

The surgeon reaches the appendiceal base and uses small spreads with a dissecting instrument to create a window between the appendiceal base and the mesoappendix. The surgeon employs a technique of sweeping up with one hand while pushing down with the other to bluntly enlarge the window, allowing for safe removal of the remainder of the mesoappendix using an energy device.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety by lifting the appendix, tenting up the cecum, and providing a clear view of the terminal ileum. The stapler is prepared with a one millimeter closed staple height to minimize bleeding while ensuring a secure closure.

The surgeon places the staple line on the cecum near the appendiceal base due to a perforation close to this area, ensuring the staple line is intact and avoiding firing staples across inflamed or unhealthy tissue.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis and gently irrigates the right lower quadrant and pelvis. The omentum is placed over the staple line for additional protection.
The surgeon places the appendix into an endoscopic retrieval bag and removes it from the patient's abdomen, marking the completion of the appendectomy procedure.
